In July 2024, it was reported that Peru as part of a revitalization program for the combat sections of the Peruvian Air Force (FAP) was considering the Rafale as one of the contenders for a recently launched contract for fighters.[397] General Carlos Enrique Chávez Cateriano, the commanding general of the FAP, announced on 8 July 2024 that a competition had been launched and the Rafale was one of two leading contenders, with the other leading contender being the KAI KF-21 Boramae.[398]

Saudi Arabia

[edit]

In February 2022, La Tribune reported that Saudi Arabia is interested in the Rafale,[399] then reported in December 2022 that Saudi Arabia would need between 100 and 200 fighters.[400] In October 2023, Saudi Arabian authorities officially asked the French company Dassault Aviation to send a quote and a proposed delivery schedule for 54 Rafale F4 combat aircraft.[401]

Ukraine

[edit]

On 17 November 2025, the presidents of Ukraine and France signed a letter of intent on the purchase of up to 100 Rafale jets over a 10–year period.[402] No schedule was given on the first delivery, with French officials citing a 3-year pilot training timeline.[403]

Uzbekistan

[edit]

On 26 November 2023, French President Emmanuel Macron offered Rafale to both Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan governments, according to La Tribune.[404] Scramble reported that Uzbekistan is interested in buying 24 Rafales, citing the source in France government.[405]

Failed bids

[edit]

The Rafale has been marketed for export to various countries. Various commentators and industry sources have highlighted the high cost of the aircraft as detrimental to the Rafale's sales prospects. Its acquisition cost is roughly US$100 million (2010),[406] while its operational cost hovers around US$16,500 (2012) for every flight-hour.[407] The Saab JAS 39 Gripen, in comparison, costs only US$4,700 per flight-hour to operate.[407] According to a 2009 article by the Institute for Defense Studies and Analysis, unlike the American government and its relationship with Boeing and Lockheed Martin, the lack of communication between the French government and Dassault has hampered a worldwide cooperative sales effort, as demonstrated by the case with Morocco in 2007.[408]

Bangladesh

[edit]

In March 2020, La Tribune reported that France's Minister of the Armed Forces, Florence Parly, promoted the Rafale's performance to Bangladeshi Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina, who is also Minister of Defense.[409][410] As of 2025, Bangladesh was in the process of acquiring the Eurofighter Typhoon.[411]

Belgium

[edit]
Main article: Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II procurement § Belgium

In 2009, Belgium suggested that they may buy F-35s in the 2020s to replace Belgium's 34 F-16A/B MLU fleet.[412] An article published in Belgian newspaper L'Avenir on 19 April 2015 speculated that, if the nuclear strike role via Belgium's Nuclear sharing policy were retained in the request for proposals, Belgium would be almost forced to buy the F-35 as to maintain this role.[413][414] Belgium officially launched its F-16 replacement program in March 2017, issuing requests for proposals to three European and two US manufacturers: Boeing Defense, Space & Security, Lockheed Martin, Dassault, Eurofighter GmbH and Saab Group, offering the F/A-18E/F Super Hornet, F-35 Lightning II, Rafale, Eurofighter Typhoon and Saab JAS 39 Gripen respectively.[415][416] On 25 October 2018, Belgium officially selected the offer for 34 F-35As; government officials stated that it had come down to price, and that "The offer from the Americans was the best in all seven evaluation criteria". The total purchasing price for the aircraft and support until 2030 totaled €4 billion, €600 million cheaper than the budgeted €4.6 billion.[417][418] In April 2020, the first F-35 contract was signed, with deliveries to begin in 2023.[419]

Brazil

[edit]
Main article: Saab JAS 39 Gripen § Brazil

In June 2008, the Brazilian Air Force issued a request for information on the following aircraft: F/A-18E/F Super Hornet, F-16 Fighting Falcon, Rafale, Su-35, Gripen NG and Eurofighter Typhoon.[420] In October 2008, the service selected three finalists for F-X2 – Dassault Rafale, Gripen NG and Boeing F/A-18E/F.[421] On 5 January 2010, media reports stated that the final evaluation report by the Brazilian Air Force placed the Gripen ahead of the other two contenders based on unit and operating costs.[422][423] In February 2011, Brazilian President Dilma Rousseff had reportedly decided in favour of the F/A-18.[424] After Edward Snowden's revelation that the NSA had been intercepting Rouseff's private communications, and her ensuing fury, the Brazilian government selected the Gripen NG in December 2013 in a US$5 billion deal to equip the air force.[425][426]

Canada

[edit]
Main article: Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II Canadian procurement

The Rafale was amongst various fighters proposed to replace the Royal Canadian Air Force's McDonnell Douglas CF-18 Hornet.[427] In 2005, a report compiled by Canada's Department of Defence reviewing aircraft noted concerns over the Rafale's interoperability with US forces; Dassault had also been unable to confirm engine performance during cold weather conditions.[428] In July 2010, the Canadian government announced the F-35 as the CF-18's replacement; the nation was already a partner in the Joint Strike Fighter program since 1997 and a Tier 3 partner for the F-35 since 2002.[429][430] In December 2012, the Canadian government announced that the F-35 buy had been abandoned due to cost rises and that a fresh procurement process would begin.[431] In January 2013, Dassault responded to Canada's request for information.[432] Various aircraft were considered, including the F-35.[433] In January 2014, Dassault offered a contract with full technology transfer, allowing Canada to perform its own support and upgrades, thereby lowering long-term service costs.[434][435] In November 2018, Dassault withdrew from the competition, reportedly over interoperability and intelligence sharing requirements, particularly with the US, complicated by France's lack of involvement in the Five Eyes intelligence-sharing group.[436][437]

Colombia

[edit]

In June 2022, La Tribune reported Dassault made an offer for 15 fighters and 9 in option for the Colombian Air Force. Colombia was interested in used ones, but France denied, taking into consideration it already sold 24 jets to Croatia and Greece.[438] On 21 December 2022, the Colombian government announced that they had shortlisted the Rafale for a potential 16 aircraft order to replace their aging Kfir.[439] Nevertheless, on 3 January 2023, Colombia and Dassault explained they could not come to an agreement, mainly because of the high price-tag of the planes. On 1 April, Colombia issued a new RFP for new planes, with the Rafale, the Gripen and the F-16 as favorites.[440] On 3 April 2025, Colombia announced it had selected the Gripen E/F to replace its Kfir fleet in a deal expected to include 16 to 24 units.[441][442]

Finland

[edit]
Main article: Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II procurement § Finland

In June 2015, a working group set up by the Finnish MoD proposed starting the HX Fighter Program to replace the Finnish Air Force's current fleet of F/A-18 Hornets. The group recognises five potential types: Boeing F/A-18E/F Super Hornet, Dassault Rafale, Eurofighter Typhoon, Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II and Saab JAS 39 Gripen E/F.[443] In December 2015, the Finnish MoD informed Great Britain, France, Sweden and the US informing them of the launch of the HX Fighter Program to replace the Hornet fleet, which will be decommissioned by 2025, with multi-role fighters; the Rafale is mentioned as a potential fighter.[444] The request for information was sent in early 2016; five responses were received in November 2016.[445] In December 2021, the Finnish newspaper Iltalehti reported that several foreign and security policy sources had confirmed the Finnish Defense Forces' recommendation of the F-35 as Finland's next fighter due to its "capability and expected long lifespan".[446][447]

Kuwait

[edit]

In February 2009, French President Nicolas Sarkozy announced that Kuwait was considering buying up to 28 Rafales.[448] In October 2009, during a visit to Paris, the Kuwaiti Defence Minister expressed interest in the Rafale and said that he was awaiting Dassault's terms.[449] Islamist lawmakers in the Kuwaiti national assembly threatened to block such a purchase, accusing the Defence Minister of lack of transparency and being manipulated by business interests.[450] In January 2012, the French Defence Minister said that both Kuwait and Qatar were waiting to see if the UAE first purchased the Rafale and that Kuwait would look to buy 18–22 Rafales.[451] However, on 11 September 2015, Eurofighter announced that an agreement had been reached with Kuwait to buy 28 Typhoons.[452][453]

Singapore

[edit]
Main article: Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II procurement § Singapore

In 2005, the Republic of Singapore Air Force launched its Next Generation Fighter (NGF) programme to replace its ageing A-4SU Super Skyhawks. Several options were considered and the Defence Science & Technology Agency (DSTA) conducted a detailed technical assessment, simulations and other tests to determine the final selection. This reduced the list of competitors to the Rafale and the F-15SG Strike Eagle. In December 2005, Singapore ordered 12 F-15SGs.[454] According to Defense Industry Daily, key reasons for the selection were that, despite the Rafale's superior aerodynamics, it had insufficient range, weapons, and sensor integration.[455]

Switzerland

[edit]
Main article: Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II procurement § Switzerland

In February 2007, Switzerland was reportedly considering the Rafale and other fighters to replace its ageing Northrop F-5 Tiger IIs.[456] A one-month evaluation started in October 2008 at Emmen Airforce Base, consisting of approximately 30 evaluation flights; the Rafale, along with the JAS 39 Gripen and the Typhoon, were evaluated.[457] Although a leaked Swiss Air Force evaluation report revealed that the Rafale won the competition on technical grounds,[N 6] on 30 November 2011, the Swiss Federal Council announced plans to buy 22 Gripen NGs due to its lower acquisition and maintenance costs.[459] Due to a referendum, this purchase never happened.

In March 2018, Swiss officials named contenders in its Air 2030 program: The Rafale, Saab Gripen, Eurofighter Typhoon, Boeing F/A-18E/F Super Hornet and Lockheed Martin F-35.[460][461] In October 2018, the Swiss Air Force was reportedly limited to buying a single-engine fighter for budgetary reasons.[462] In May 2019, the Rafale performed demonstration flights at Payerne Air Base for comparison against other bids.[463] On 30 June 2021, the Swiss Federal Council proposed to Parliament the acquisition of 36 F-35As[464][465] at a cost of up to 6 billion Swiss francs (US$6.5 billion), citing the aircraft's cost- and combat-effectiveness.[466] However, it was later confirmed that the costs are capped for a period of just 10 years.[467] The Liberal Greens have promised to examine the F-35's environmental impact.[468] The Swiss anti-military group GSoA intended to contest the purchase in another national referendum supported by the Green Party of Switzerland and the Social Democratic Party of Switzerland (which previously managed to block the Gripen).[469][470] In August 2022, they registered the initiative, with 120,000 people having signed in less than a year (with 100,000 required).[471]

On 15 September 2022, the Swiss National council gave the Federal council permission to sign the purchase deal, with a time limit for signing of March 2023.[472] The deal to buy 36 F-35A was signed on 19 September 2022, with deliveries to commence in 2027 and conclude by 2030, bypassing the popular initiative.[473][474]

UAE

[edit]

On 4 April 2026, during the 2026 Iran war, the UAE announced its withdrawal from the financing deal with France of Rafale F5. The UAE was to provide finance of about €3.5 billion to the program. According to sources this is due to France's refusal to provide access for local companies to technology. This means that the French Ministry of Armed Forces will have to fund the whole program.[475]

Other bids

[edit]

In 2002, the Republic of Korea Air Force chose the F-15K Slam Eagle over the Dassault Rafale, Eurofighter Typhoon and Sukhoi Su-35 for its 40 aircraft F-X Phase 1 fighter competition.[476]

In January 2007, the French newspaper Journal du Dimanche reported that Libya sought 13 to 18 Rafales "in a deal worth as much as US$3.24 billion".[477] In December 2007, Saif al-Islam Gaddafi declared Libya's interest in the Rafale,[478] but no order was placed. French Rafales later attacked targets in Libya as part of the international military intervention during the 2011 Libyan civil war.[479]

In late 2007, La Tribune reported that a prospective US$2.85 billion sale to Morocco had fallen through, the government selecting the F-16C/D instead.[480][481] While French Defense Minister Hervé Morin labelled it as overly sophisticated and too costly, defense analysts have said that miscalculations of the DGA's offer price and hesitations over financing were detrimental to the negotiations.[481][482]

In February 2009, France offered Rafales to Oman to replace its ageing fleet of SEPECAT Jaguars.[483] In December 2012, Oman placed an order for 12 Typhoons.[484][485]

Variants

[edit]

French variants

[edit]
Rafale A
Technology demonstrator, first flew in 1986.[7]
Rafale D
Dassault used this designation (D for discret) in the early 1990s to emphasise the new semi-stealthy design features.[43]
Rafale B
Two-seater version for the French Air and Space Force.[35]
Rafale C
Same as Rafale B but single-seat version for the French Air and Space Force.[35]
Rafale M
Similar to Rafale C, but with modifications to allow operations from CATOBAR – equipped aircraft carriers. For carrier operations, the M model has a strengthened airframe, longer nose gear leg to provide a more nose-up attitude, larger tailhook between the engines, and a built-in boarding ladder. Consequently, the Rafale M weighs about 500 kg (1,100 lb) more than the Rafale C.[35][39] It is the only non-US fighter type cleared to operate from the decks of US carriers, using catapults and their arresting gear, as demonstrated in 2008 when six Rafales from Flottille 12F integrated into the USS Theodore Roosevelt Carrier Air Wing interoperability exercise.[486]
Rafale N
Originally called the Rafale BM, was a planned missile-only two-seater version for the Aéronavale.[134] Budgetary constraints have been cited as grounds for its cancellation.[487]
Rafale R
Proposed reconnaissance-oriented variant.[83]

Export variants

[edit]
Rafale DM
Two-seater version for the Egyptian Air Force.[488]
Rafale EM
Single-seat version for the Egyptian Air Force.[489]
Rafale DH
Two-seater version for the Indian Air Force.[490]
Rafale EH
Single-seat version for the Indian Air Force.[491]
Rafale MH
Single-seater version for the Indian Navy; customized carrier-capable variant of the Rafale M, adapted for STOBAR and Indian weapon systems.[492]
Rafale DQ
Two-seater version for the Qatar Emiri Air Force.[493]
Rafale EQ
Single-seat version for the Qatar Emiri Air Force.[493]
Rafale DG
Two-seater version for the Hellenic Air Force.[494]
Rafale EG
Single-seat version for the Hellenic Air Force.[495]
Rafale DI
Two-seater version for the Indonesian Air Force.
Rafale EI
Single-seat version for the Indonesian Air Force.

Operators

[edit]

[edit]

Current operators

[edit]
Croatia
  • Croatian Air Force – 12 ex-French C/B F3-R Rafales, consisting of 10 single-seat C F3-R and 2x two-seat B F3-R fighters. The first six were delivered on 25 April 2024 (2 B + 4 C) with the remaining six single-seaters were to be delivered in 2025.[needs update][496][347] In October 2023, Croatia officially acquired the first aircraft at a ceremony at Mont-de-Marsan Air Base.[348][349] The 12th Rafale was delivered on 25 April 2025.[350] On 8 December 2025 in France, Croatian Defence Minister, and the French Minister for Armed Forces signed a contract Letter of Intent for further upgrading 12 Rafale aircraft from the Rafale F3-R standard to enhanced capabilities of the Rafale F4 standard.[351]
    • 191st Fighter Aircraft Squadron
Egypt
  • Egyptian Air Force – 54 ordered with 32 Rafales delivered as of 2019[update].[177][497][181]
France
In 2025 France informed that it plan to order a total of 286 Rafale by 2035 following the budget plan for 2026, with an additional order of up to 61 units. The previous orders was of 234 units. This order was made to reach and maintain the total number of Rafale to 225 active units by 2035, since France sold to Croatia (12) and Greece (12) from its own stock and lost 2 units in the 2024 collision crash and to replace older models that entered service in 1999.[498][91] As of 2026, 189 units were delivered to the French Armed Forces, with 12 units sold to Greece and another 12 to Croatia, France currently operates around 165 Rafale. 124 in the Air and Space Force and 41 in the Navy.[499]Once the deliveries of 286 units completed, France is expected to field 225 units. 185 for the Air and Space Force and 40 for the Navy. Option for up to 30 additional units to complement the 225 was mentioned by the Minister of the Defense in early 2025. Potentially raising the total fleet to 255 units.[500][501][502][503][490][504][505]
  • French Air and Space Force – ~124;[506][507] flying units include:
    • Saint-Dizier – Robinson Air Base
      • Escadron de Chasse 2/4 La Fayette (2018–present)[508] nuclear strike
      • Escadron de Chasse 1/7 Provence (2006–2016)[116] multirole fighter
      • Escadron de Chasse 1/4 Gascogne (2009–present)[116] nuclear strike
      • Escadron de Transformation Rafale 3/4 Aquitaine (October 2010–present, Rafale Operational Conversion Unit (OCU) jointly operated by French Air and Space Force and French Naval Aviation)[116]
    • Mont-de-Marsan Air Base
      • Escadron de Chasse 2/30 Normandie-Niemen (2012–present)[116] multirole fighter
      • Escadron de Chasse 3/30 Lorraine (2016–present)[116] multirole fighter
      • Escadron de chasse et d'expérimentation 1/30 Côte d'Argent (2004–present) tactics development and evaluation
    • Orange-Caritat Air Base
      • Escadron de Chasse 1/5 Vendée (2024–present)[9] multirole fighter
    • Al Dhafra Air Base, UAE
      • Escadron de Chasse 3/30 Lorraine (2010–2016)[5] multirole fighter
      • Escadron de Chasse 1/7 Provence (2016–present) multirole fighter
  • French Navy – 46 delivered, 41 active[509]
    • Naval Air Base Landivisiau
      • Flottille 11F (2011–present)[116] multirole carrier fighter
      • Flottille 12F (2001–present)[1] multirole carrier fighter
      • Flottille 17F (2016–present)[510] multirole carrier fighter
Greece
  • Hellenic Air Force – Greece ordered 18 Rafales F-3R in 2020,[511] and an additional 6 in 2021.[512] The first was delivered on 21 July 2021.[513] All 24 have been delivered to the Hellenic Air Force as of January 2025.[514][515][516][517][518]
    • Tanagra Air Base
      • 332nd All Weather Squadron (Hawks)[494]
India
  • Indian Air Force – 36 Rafale F3-Rs ordered (28 Rafale EH and 8 Rafale DH), all delivered by July 2022.[519][520][521] A deal for additional 114 units of F4 variants was accorded Acceptance of Necessity by Defence Acquisition Council in February 2026.[522][523]
    • Ambala AFS[524]
      • No. 17 Squadron (Golden Arrows)[525]
    • Hasimara AFS[524]
      • No. 101 Squadron (Falcons)[526]
  • Indian Navy – 26 Rafale F4s (22 Rafale-M and 4 twin-seater) on order to be delivered by 2030.[323]
Indonesia
  • Indonesian Air Force – 42 Rafale F4s ordered with 6 delivered as of May 2026.[384][527][528][529] The first batch of three Rafales were ceremonially received on 28 November 2025,[381] and delivered on 23 January 2026.[383]
    • 31st Air Wing
      • 1st Air Squadron (Equatorial Eagles) – Supadio AFB, Pontianak (planned)
      • 12th Air Squadron (Black Panther) – Roesmin Nurjadin AFB, Pekanbaru
      • 16th Air Squadron (Rydders) – Roesmin Nurjadin AFB, Pekanbaru (planned)
Qatar
  • Qatar Emiri Air Force – 36 ordered, all delivered. Qatar ordered 24 of the fighters in 2015, and 12 more in 2018. It also has an option to order 36 more.[530] As of 2023, all aircraft were delivered.[531]
    • Dukhan / Tamim Airbase
      • 1st Fighter Squadron 'Al Adiyat'
United Arab Emirates
  • United Arab Emirates Air Force – 80 Rafale F4s on order.[532] The first was ceremonially delivered on 29 January 2025.[367]
Future operators
Serbia
  • Serbian Air Force and Air Defence – 12 aircraft (F4 version) ordered in 2024 with deliveries to be completed by 2029. [533][534] The deal signifies a strategic shift from Russian/Soviet fighter aircraft towards French/NATO ones.
Ukraine
  • In early February 2026, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced plans for Ukraine to acquire up to 100 Rafales from France and 150 Saab JAS 39 Gripens from Sweden as part of a major modernization of the Ukrainian Air Force. These agreements, framed through letters of intent and bilateral memoranda, represent one of the largest proposed fighter procurement efforts in Ukraine's history and signify a shift away from Soviet/Russian-designed aircraft.

Notable accidents

[edit]
  • On 6 December 2007, a French Air Force twin-seat Rafale crashed during a training flight. The pilot, who suffered from spatial disorientation, died in the accident.[535]
  • On 24 September 2009, after unarmed test flights, two French Navy Rafales returning to the aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle, collided in mid-air about 30 kilometres (19 mi) from the town of Perpignan in southwest France. One test pilot, identified as François Duflot, died in the accident, while the other was rescued.[536]
  • On 28 November 2010, a Rafale from the carrier Charles de Gaulle crashed in the Arabian Sea. This aircraft was supporting Allied operations in Afghanistan. The pilot ejected safely and was rescued by a rescue helicopter from the carrier. Later reports said the engines stopped after being starved of fuel due to confusion by the pilot in switching fuel tanks.[537]
  • On 2 July 2012, during a joint exercise, a Rafale from the carrier Charles de Gaulle plunged into the Mediterranean Sea. The pilot ejected safely and was recovered by an American search and rescue helicopter from the carrier USS Dwight D. Eisenhower.[538]
  • On 14 August 2024, two French Rafale B two-seaters collided over Colombey-les-Belles. While one pilot had ejected before crashing into the ground, the trainee and instructor of the second aircraft were reported missing. Both aircraft were based in Saint-Dizier – Robinson Air Base. By 15 August, the death of the missing pilots was announced.[539][540]

Specifications (Rafale C, B and M)

[edit]

Data from Dassault Aviation,[541] Superfighters,[542] French Navy,[543] International Directory of Military Aircraft[544]

General characteristics

  • Crew: 1 or 2
  • Length: 15.27 m (50 ft 1 in)
  • Wingspan: 10.90 m (35 ft 9 in)
  • Height: 5.34 m (17 ft 6 in)
  • Wing area: 45.7 m2 (492 sq ft)
  • Empty weight: 10,300 kg (22,708 lb) (B)[89][541]
9,850 kg (21,720 lb) (C)[89][541]
10,600 kg (23,400 lb) (M)[89][541]
  • Gross weight: 15,000 kg (33,069 lb)
  • Max takeoff weight: 24,500 kg (54,013 lb)
  • Fuel capacity: 4,700 kg (10,362 lb) internal for single-seater (C); 4,400 kg (9,700 lb) for two-seater (B)
  • With external tanks: 4,700 kg (10,362 lb) internal and 6,700 kg (14,771 lb) external[541]
  • Maximum fuel: (C): 16,550 L (4,370 US gal; 3,640 imp gal) 5,750 L (1,520 US gal; 1,260 imp gal) internal + 2,300 L (610 US gal; 510 imp gal) in 2x conformal tanks[545]
  • Powerplant: 2 × Snecma M88-4e turbofans, 50.00 kN (11,240 lbf) thrust each [546] dry, 75.00 kN (16,860 lbf) with afterburner

Performance

  • Maximum speed: 1,912 km/h (1,188 mph, 1,032 kn) [547] / Mach 1.8[547] at high altitude
1,390 km/h; 860 mph; 750 kn / Mach 1.1 at low altitude
  • Supercruise: on 4 missiles and a 1250-liter belly droptank[548]
    • Mach 1.4 supercruise on Rafale M (navy) version with 6 MICA air-to-air missiles[549]
  • Combat range: 1,056 km (656 mi, 570 nmi) hi-lo-hi, on penetration mission with three tanks (5,700 L combined), two SCALP-EG and four MICA AAMs[citation needed]
  • Ferry range: 3,700 km (2,300 mi, 2,000 nmi) with 3 drop tanks
  • Service ceiling: 15,835 m (51,952 ft)
  • g limits: +9 −3.6 (+11 in emergencies)[77][550][551]
  • Rate of climb: 304.8 m/s (60,000 ft/min)
  • Wing loading: 328 kg/m2 (67 lb/sq ft)

Armament

  • Guns: 1× 30 mm (1.2 in) GIAT 30/M791 autocannon with 125 rounds
  • Hardpoints: 14 External hardpoints for Air and Space Force versions (Rafale B/C), 13 for Navy version (Rafale M) with a capacity of 9,500 kg (20,900 lb) external fuel and ordnance, with provisions to carry combinations of:
    • Missiles:
      • Air-to-air:
        • MICA EM and IR (most widely used air-to-air missiles on Rafale; MICA-EM and MICA-IR both used for short-range and also for medium-range BVR combat)
        • Meteor
        • Magic II
      • Air-to-surface:
        • MBDA Apache
        • MBDA Storm Shadow/SCALP-EG
        • AASM-Hammer (SBU-38/54/64)
        • GBU-12 Paveway II, GBU-16 Paveway II, GBU-22 Paveway III, GBU-24 Paveway III, GBU-49 Enhanced Paveway II
        • AS-30L
        • Mark 82[552]
        • MBDA AM 39-Exocet air-launched anti-ship missile (one AM 39-Exocet at the central under-fuselage hardpoint)[553][554]
        • MBDA Stratus cruise/anti-ship missile (in development)
      • Nuclear Deterrence:
        • ASMP-A nuclear missile
        • ASN4G hypersonic nuclear missile (in development)
    • Other:
      • Thales Damocles targeting pod
      • Thales AREOS (Airborne Recce Observation System) reconnaissance pod[555]
      • Thales TALIOS multi-function targeting pod[556]
      • Up to 5 drop tanks
      • Buddy–buddy refueling pod[89]

Avionics

  • Thales RBE2-AA AESA radar
  • Thales SPECTRA Electronic Warfare (EW) system
  • Thales/SAGEM Optronique Secteur Frontal (OSF) Infrared search and track (IRST) system

See also

[edit]
  • Aviation portal
  • Fourth-generation jet fighter

Aircraft of comparable role, configuration, and era

  • Shenyang J-15
  • Boeing F/A-18E/F Super Hornet
  • Eurofighter Typhoon
  • Mikoyan MiG-29K
  • HAL TEDBF

  • List of fighter aircraft
  • List of military aircraft of France

Notes

[edit]
  1. ^ Quote: "Dassault demands design leadership as the price for European co-operation."[14]
  2. ^ Quote: "Longitudinal stability is moderately negative with a full fly-by-wire digital control system. The system is quadruple redundant with three digital channels and one separately designed analog channel. Design independence between channels is pivotal in preventing fatal flaws simultaneously affecting several channels due to software misconceptions."[79]
  3. ^ Quote: "The core of the enhanced capabilities of the RAFALE lies in a new Modular Data Processing Unit (MDPU). It is composed of up to 18 flight line-replaceable modules, each with a processing power 50 times higher than that of the 2084 XRI-type computer fitted on the early versions of Mirage 2000-5."[79]
  4. ^ Quote: "SPECTRA provides all-weather reliable long-range detection, identification and location of threats, short response times and cutting-edge defensive measures based on combinations of jamming, decoying and evasive manoeuvres and on state-of-the-art technologies such as DRFM (Digital Radio Frequency Memory) signal processing."[79]
  5. ^ Quote: "According to DGA, Areos provides day identification capabilities that are two-and-a-half times better than those of the Mirage F1CR's Presto "wet-film" system and 8 times better than those of the legacy SDS250 photo pod of the Super-Étendard."[103]
  6. ^ Quote: "The Rafale was the clear winner of the SAF evaluation, with the Eurofighter second, but the Swiss government opted for the cheaper Gripen package."[458]

  • "Air Superiority". Flight International. 124 (3892). Surrey, UK: Reed Business Information: 1509. 4–10 December 1983. ISSN 0015-3710. Archived from the original on 1 November 2010.
  • "How to Survive a Dogfight Blackout". New Scientist. 109. London: Reed Business Information: 33. 20 March 1986. ISSN 0262-4079.
  • "Military Aircraft of the World". Flight International. 140 (4281). London: Reed Business Information: 34. 21–27 August 1991. ISSN 0015-3710. Archived from the original on 25 August 2013. Retrieved 15 February 2013.
  • "France orders more Rafales". Flight International. 145 (4410). London: Reed Business Information: 27. 2–8 March 1994. ISSN 0015-3710. Archived from the original on 20 December 2014.

  • Carbonel, Jean-Christophe (2016). French Secret Projects. Vol. 1: Post War Fighters. Manchester, UK: Crecy Publishing. ISBN 978-1-91080-900-6.

  • Eden, Paul, ed. (2004). The Encyclopedia of Modern Military Aircraft. London: Amber Books. ISBN 978-1-904687-84-9.
  • Fiszer, Michal (1 June 2005). "Storm warning: the Dassault Rafale is poised to become Europe's premier fighter-bomber". Journal of Electronic Defense. 28 (6). Association of Old Crows: 40–51. ISSN 0192-429X.
  • Forsberg, Randall, ed. (1994). The Arms Production Dilemma: Contraction and Restraint in the World Combat Aircraft Industry. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. ISBN 978-0-262-56085-6.
  • Frawley, Gerard (2002). The International Directory of Military Aircraft. Fyshwick, AU: Aerospace Publications. ISBN 978-1-875671-55-7.
  • Gaines, Mike (4–10 June 1989). "Rafale: Dassault's New Dawn". Flight International. 135 (4168). London: Reed Business Information: 80–88. ISSN 0015-3710. Archived from the original on 20 January 2014.
  • Jarret, DN (2005). Cockpit Engineering. Surrey, UK: Ashgate Publishing. ISBN 978-0-7546-1751-8.
  • Lenorovitz, Jeffrey (23 June 1986). "Rafale Serves as Testbed for French Systems". Aviation Week & Space Technology. 124 (25). New York: McGraw-Hill: 79. ISSN 0005-2175.
  • ——— (5 November 1990). "Rafale Debut Opens Way for Flight Test Program". Aviation Week & Space Technology. 133 (19). New York: McGraw-Hill: 20–22. ISSN 0005-2175.
  • Lorell, Mark; Hoffman, Donna (1989). The Use of Prototypes in Selected Foreign Fighter Aircraft Development Programs – Rafale, EAP, Lavi, and Gripen. RAND Corporation. ISBN 0-8330-0970-2.
  • MacKenzie, Christina (19–25 March 2002). "Rafale Readies". Flight International. 161 (4824). London: Reed Business Information: 36–37. ISSN 0015-3710. Retrieved 19 October 2013.
  • Richardson, Doug (2001). Stealth Warplanes. Grand Rapids, MI: Zenith Imprint. ISBN 978-0-7603-1051-9.
  • Sedbon, Gilbert (26 January – 1 February 1986). "Rafale partners sought formally". Flight International. 129 (3996). London: Reed Business Information: 11. ISSN 0015-3710. Archived from the original on 11 April 2013. Retrieved 13 January 2013.
  • ——— (6–12 July 1986). "Dassault flies Rafale demonstrator". Flight International. 130 (4019). London: Reed Business Information: 4. ISSN 0015-3710. Archived from the original on 11 April 2013. Retrieved 12 January 2013.
  • Sedbon, Gilbert (14–20 February 1988). "Crouzet controls by voice". Flight International. 133 (4101). London: Reed Business Information: 30–31. ISSN 0015-3710. Archived from the original on 27 August 2013. Retrieved 19 October 2013.
  • ———; Norris, Guy (10–16 April 1991). "Power to Progress". Flight International. 139 (4262). London: Reed Business Information: 32–36. ISSN 0015-3710. Archived from the original on 13 January 2010.
  • Sparaco, Pierre (18 November 1996). "France Seeks Early Export Orders". Aviation Week & Space Technology. 145 (21). New York: McGraw-Hill: 29. ISSN 0005-2175.
  • Spick, Mike, ed. (2000). The Great Book of Modern Warplanes. St. Paul, MN: MBI Publishing. ISBN 978-1-84065-156-0.
  • Svitak, Amy; Bruneau, Caroline (8–21 June 2015). "Fasionably Late". Aviation Week & Space Technology. 177 (11). New York: McGraw-Hill: 59–60. ISSN 0005-2175.
  • Sweetman, Bill (1 November 2002). "Killer angels: France's Rafale has fire, but will glory remain a mirage?". Journal of Electronic Defense. 25 (11). Association of Old Crows: 44–48. ISSN 0192-429X.
